items.q: What is the difference between the Burgess and Hoyt urban land use models?
items.correct: Burgess (1925) describes concentric rings radiating from the CBD; Hoyt (1939) describes sectors (wedges) extending along transport routes — recognising how transport shapes urban growth.
items.misconception: Burgess and Hoyt models show the same thing in different drawings.
items.random_wrong: Burgess is for American cities and Hoyt is for European cities.
items.why: The models differ structurally: rings vs wedges. Both were derived from Chicago. Real cities have elements of both — modern models like Mann combine them with sector wedges within concentric zones.

items.q: Why do TNCs locate production in LICs?
items.correct: Lower labour costs, weaker regulation, access to raw materials, large potential markets, and government incentives like tax breaks in special economic zones.
items.misconception: Because TNCs want to develop poorer countries and lift them out of poverty.
items.random_wrong: Because LICs have better infrastructure than HICs.
items.why: Students attribute moral motives. TNCs are profit-driven; any development benefit is a side effect, not a goal — a critical distinction for evaluation questions.

items.q: What happens during an El Nino event?
items.correct: Trade winds weaken, warm water that normally pools in the western Pacific spreads east, disrupting weather patterns globally — drought in Australia, floods in South America.
items.misconception: El Nino is when the ocean gets colder near Peru.
items.random_wrong: El Nino is the name of a specific hurricane that hit Mexico.
items.why: El Nino brings WARM water to Peru — the cold-water state is La Nina. Students mix up the two phases; the name 'the Christ child' refers to warm currents at Christmas.
It's Internal to place. “External to place” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: E20. “E1” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 9. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
